next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

The reader liked his right eye better than the left. Most machines did.

It took two passes before the locker clicked. By then the freight district had settled deeper into its night noises: a generator knocking somewhere off tempo, sheet metal pinging as the cold came down, dust hissing over concrete in long low sweeps. The loading bays along the lane glowed in strips. Everything else had gone black. Forklifts slept behind chain-link with their forks lowered like tired jaws.

Spike pulled the door open and stood there a second without going in.

The place smelled the same as it had four years ago: old paper, oil gone gummy in its can, cardboard that had drunk too much dry air and given up. It wasn't memory exactly. Memory moved. This was just storage. A dead version of his life stacked and numbered and waiting for nobody.

He stepped inside, shut the door against the wind, and clicked on the penlight he had brought this time.

The beam found crates, binders, bundles of route sheets strapped with brittle bands. He'd come here the first time because the alias had flared to life and he needed to know what else might still be breathing with it. He'd expected danger and gotten paperwork. One manifest moved from the bottom of a stack to the top. One sentence in a hand he didn't know.

She needed this to live.

Five words. There was no name and no accusation. The absence needled him most. If whoever left it had written You did this, that would've been simple. If they'd written My mother or Help me or Even now, it would've been something he could push against. Instead...
open_hooks_to_carry: Faye finds Miriam at a diner and gets the full story — the Golden Ox on Ganymede, the woman in the blue dress, the scar on the left shoulder blade, the tip nobody gave at the Golden Ox
Faye realizes the woman was not her — no matching scar — but the name and the gesture were hers or were they the other woman's first
prev_tail_excerpt:
...arrived ready-made and worthless.

She let them die.

Jet cleared his throat, low and rough. "He's gonna wake up freezing if I don't."

Nobody had asked.

"Yeah," Spike said, eyes on the blanket. "Draft gets bad down there."

That was all.

It was enough to make Faye look away first.

She turned back to the porthole where Mars sat huge and bruised below them. The collarbone under her hand still felt marked from the bathroom though there was nothing there. Behind her, Jet moved around the galley shutting things down one light at a time. Spike turned the receiver another notch lower. On the deck, under the singed blanket, Ed slept with mouth open and one card still stuck to forehead while Ein pressed his nose into the wool and dreamed whatever dogs with human-grade data in their heads dreamed.

Jet switched off the galley light.

The common room went softer.

Nobody said good night. They almost never did. The ship wasn't built for that kind of family and none of them had the nerve to retrofit it.

Still, the blanket stayed where Jet had put it. Spike kept the volume low. Faye sat by the porthole until her shoulder stopped hurting from the way she'd been holding herself and then, finally, let her hand fall. By then morning felt closer than sleep did.

The Bebop drifted over Hellas with its engines cold, ticket unpaid, pantry close to bare. Under the thin wool on the deck, two small bodies breathed in different rhythms and somehow made the room feel warmer than the heaters did.

Outside, Mars kept its distance.

Inside, the old ship ticked and settled around them for the night.
state_deltas_required: Faye's lost identity — the horror of learning someone else lived a life under your name while you were frozen
Faye's understanding of her own identity fractures; the pre-cryosleep identity theft is confirmed with material detail; the other Faye becomes real through a scar, a dress, a gambling table, a tip
